transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
That's the worst feeling.
I'm very sorry for making you wait.
No, no problem.
Cristina, think, yeah, there she is.
She's just joined.
So I'll let her do a very short intro while she is sat on the train.
If you can do that, Cristina, see if the internet is old.
Let's cross your fingers, cross your toes for me.
Hey, Tyler, I'm Cristina.
Can you hear me okay?
Give me a thumbs up.
Loud and clear.
Thank you so much for joining from the train.
appreciate it.
No problem.
No problem.
Doing my best.
Yeah, so I think Oli mentioned I'll be joining Spendesk soon, towards the end of January, and I'll be taking over as head of content.
As far as I know, we'll be a very lean team.
So I think we'll be looking for a little bit of support.
I'm very passionate about AI, about content, and we're looking for someone to help with this transition.
Yeah, hopefully.
that's
Ollie's going to add in more detail.
Nailed it.
Awesome.
Yeah, great to meet you, Cristina.
Tell me a little bit more about like how you've used AI to create content.
That's a big part of what we do.
So very curious.
Yes.
So I am currently at Sona, which is a workforce management solution.
I will say that for us, thought leadership has been very important in terms of content strategy.
And this has, we also operate in an enterprise environment.
We work a lot with one-to-one ABM messaging.
So scale for content hasn't been super relevant.
So, but on the other hand, we're a team of one.
So it has been very relevant to me.
I've worked on building some very basic workflows, probably, compared to what you work on.
So for example, we do a lot of video content in the form of webinars.
So that's...
That is probably the source of some of our pieces.
And then together with our web op manager, who's worked on building some simple Zapier automations, to turn that webinar content into drafts for blogs, for social media.
But it's always been with my intention as an editor.
It's still very manual.
So I think for us, there is an understanding.
There just hasn't been enough resource attached or desire to prioritize this because we've been working in this kind of ABM motion.
So yes, I think probably goals as Spendesk will be to scale this.
It's a very different type of company.
It's a very different type of strategy.
So we're looking to kind of obviously reconcile quality and quantity.
Yeah, it makes sense.
Very similar to our approach.
I would just say that at GrowthX, we're more focused on like SEO and GEO content.
It's going to be editorial in nature as the foundation.
And then we'll also look into programmatic opportunities as we're working through editorial with you.
What's cool about our process is that in phase one, it's all strategic, but you get published articles as part of this eight-week accelerated engagement.
The reason that we focus on strategy before scale is because our workflows are only as effective as the context that we feed in.

And we've got this process that we've taken over 60 clients through where we create what we call artifacts around their company, their ICP or personas, and then their brand voice and writing guidelines.
So there's these four artifacts that we create for every client, and that allows us to really personalize and make the content as relevant to your audience as possible.
This is also going to come with analysis around...
The search opportunity and also connecting that to the right topic clusters where we should scale out content where you want to have authority.
And so if you think about, you know, company in the space that's similar, Ramp, we've published over 700 pages for Ramp and we started off with a lot of their editorial content.
You know, how do ACH payments work?
Thinking about what's the most relevant cross-linking that we should do with other content on their domain?
How can we structure the content in a format that's going to be optimized for LLMs?
And then also image generation.
We'll do cover images for each article.
We'll do the LLM visibility and monitoring so that you don't need to buy, you know, an additional tool like a Profound or a Scrunch.
We've got like an internal platform that addresses a lot of the gaps there that you get to leverage.
And then once we've seen some great signals, start to see organic traffic going in the right direction.
Well, then we can really think about programmatic use cases.
And so for Ramp, we created this vendor directory where, you know, Ramp wants to be the authority in business spending.
And so we took the most popular B2B software tools, and then we created these pages where we programmatically leveraged Ramp's internal spend data to populate these interactive charts.

But then we also thought about, well, you know, when you get to the bottom of this page, and you can see, like, there's tables in our content also, you know, people want to know what Ramp alternatives are out there.
I'm sorry, what Airtable alternatives are out there.
What about Airtable compared to the most popular tools?
And so it's not, you know, maybe, like, the most obvious at the beginning.

But then as you think about, you know, who is their audience and what is their strategy, Ramp being able to show up in a search of Airtable versus ClickUp is actually highly relevant to how they want to be seen, you know, in the market.
By the people who aren't using their product today.
And so this is just like a glimpse of like some content we've created for very similar, you know, company to Spendesk.
So where did you start with building the tone of voice, the ICP information, what kind of like phase zero before you get started on all of this?
Yeah, amazing question.
So we'll ingest, I mean, these sales conversations are like a good starting point, right?
Like I need to get as much context around like, who are you creating content for?
What gaps are you trying to solve for?
What topics do you want to create content around?
Who are your main competitors?
And then our team from there ingest resources.
So we'll ingest your brand guidelines, your tone and voice docs, any positioning or analysis that you've done, product marketing one pagers.
And then we've got a process where we do an hour kickoff and dive super deep into these standard questions that we ask.
But we take the information that I get to go deeper on those questions.
deeper those questions.
do a product deep dive with usually someone in the product marketing org or the best person to give us a demo.
And then we'll do a CMS deep dive so that we understand the nuances of how we would stage and publish.
We stage and publish for most of our clients.
And so, you know, that process allows us to, when we get to the actual artifacts, and I'll go into the, you can see my screen okay?
I don't know if you're looking from a phone or a laptop.
Oh, good.
All good?
Cristina, this is good for you?
Good on my side.
Good on my side.
Perfect.
So let's take Engine, for example.
Engine's in the business travel space, and we've published all these different guides for them.
So, you know, this is our internal platform.
We're adding a lot of different pieces to this, and it's internal today, but we're opening it up to our clients pretty soon.
And if you look at the artifacts for Engine, you know, we understood that it was important to verticalize their content.
So we go super deep thinking about AI search and how everyone can prompt in different ways and variations and continue the conversation.
You know, they go after the operations manager, the finance manager, and the project manager.
So we are trying to basically get as relevant and specific, but also thorough into all their jobs to be done, right?
What are they thinking about that could be connected to your products and services?
And then this feeds into every piece of content that we create, why they choose Engine.
There's customer stories that we're leveraging for Engine.
There's, you know, we'll create unique artifacts over time.
And then we'll also have specific instructions.
Okay, how do they want to mention or not mention competitors?
What are the specific terminology and good and bad examples of content that we create?
And so these artifacts are key in addition to the knowledge base where we can ingest...
Resources, and also store the agentic research that we do.
So if you look at Augment Code, we've got all of, you know, Augment Code, we've created all these guides for Augment Code.
They're kind of technical AI enterprise coding.
And we've got this research in addition to some different resources, and we store it all in a knowledge graph to optimize for retrieval.
And so it's the combination of these two pieces that feed into every piece of content that we create.
Do you have any questions?
I know it definitely gets in the weeds, and I'm happy to, you know, dive deep.
Just a question for me, this, because I feel like there's many different ways that I would interpret knowledge graph in the context of Spendesk and you guys potentially working for us, right?
Which would be...

So how does within the knowledge graph, how do we feedback signals of kind of what works versus what doesn't as judged by the users, not just by the content that we end up outputting, if that makes sense?
Does that kind of fit into this or is it a completely separate thing that, you know?
So the only thing I don't understand there is like in terms of the users, like which users, like the people reading the content or the people leveraging the platform to create the content?
So, yeah.
So I guess what the end goal here is that the model, whatever we create here, improves as well over time.

So like we have a starting point, but then we kind of keep the rhythm going, keep that wheel turning through kind of refining, improving, because we're, you know, we have, I guess the first two months would give us a starting.
But what we don't know is like, oh, did that content specifically show up in, you know, LLMs or did this content specifically get the highest engagement rate?
And is there a way that there's like a self-learning aspect to what we create here?
Or is this something completely separate to, guess, what you're showing us here?
I see.
I see.
So we would use this internally, our team, right?
Over time, the goal is definitely to help you start to leverage our platform to execute on these workflows through your team.
But right now, think of us as like performing a white glove service and using our internal platform to execute those services with experts in the loop.
And so, you know, to your point, right, we are setting up a Looker dashboard where we're tracking all of the different LLM referrals in addition to content engagement.
We're focused.
We're looking very closely.
At your organic traffic and how our content is performing against that traffic and what converts.
And so it's more like that's more of the human service, right?

Is that you get the performance data is kind of like it's kind of moved away from away out of that tool into other tools with human expertise that we get to kind of figure out what's working, what's not.
Exactly.

We use the workflows and the knowledge graph and the artifacts to create like the highest quality content that we can against the strategy that we're setting up before we even think about the workflows and the context engineering.

And the strategy is all coming from, right, SEO experts from our team doing keyword analysis, connecting it to the competitor content, looking at the opportunities to refresh content and where, you know, we have workflows to refresh content.
And then once, once we really.
We understand, like, your personas, their jobs to be done, how your product is, you know, differentiated against your competition, which topic clusters that we should focus on.
Then we move into the content creation.
But what's great about, you know, our process is that these artifacts are living documents.
And so, you know, as we're seeing things work well and what's not working, you know, our team, they manually make changes in the platform to the content before we send it.
Cristina, to your team, to review.
But we also think about, well, how can we improve these artifacts so that our quality gets higher?
And, you know, to your point, Olly, the research that we're doing, it's improving over time.
And it's making that knowledge graph, like, have a wider breadth of information so that, you the system is, right, getting better and better.
But to your other question, like, the data is not connected to the workflows.
It's something that our team does.
It's
So just help me in my very simple brain, help me understand the exact purpose of that knowledge.
Knowledge graphics are very interesting.
And I've seen that used in SEO, right?
Where it's like the screaming frog kind of spider diagram of all content, how it's connected to clusters.
Is it the same kind of thing when I look at that knowledge graph?
Is it like to help us link content from an SEO point of view?
Or is it something that's fundamental to the AI model?
I would say that it's more about when we do research, helping us speed up the process, number one.

And number two, like making sure that our research is connected to the right sources and that we know how to like go right to those sources the next time we create a new piece of content or supplementing like the research that we're doing with the internal resources that you've already created.
It's basically to make the research.
As fast and effective as possible.
And that knowledge graph, you build out like a V1 kind of with us together.
We kind of do this knowledge graph across the whole of the category of spend management and finance.
Or is it like over just progressively over time as we're building?
What's the play there?
I would say it's not something your team really has to like worry about or collaborate with us around.
The artifacts are more important to how we write the content.
The knowledge graph is more important to how we research.
But someone goes away and I guess like how does someone go away and create that, right?
You must have a tool or something that allows you to kind of see how all these things are connected.
Do you know what mean?
Like that's quite a quite a mission to do that manually.
Yeah, I mean, the technology is definitely like taking the research and then creating the knowledge graph of different topics.
And then as we read

Write a new piece of content, it's going to do research across the internet, it's going to use specialized tools like EXA or Tavly, and then it's also going to use the existing research that we've done so that it can connect directly to those topics for the research that's going on.
So if you look at the way that we're creating content here, let's take creating an assignment as a first step.

For an assignment, we are taking a topic or a keyword, in this case it's like a comparison article, and it's not connecting to the knowledge base here, it's using the artifacts around the company and around the personas.
And then to get to this output, which is the brief for the assignment, which is the content structure, we're going through and we're connecting to...

Like SEMrush's API to look at organic results, the SERP API to look at keyword traffic and search intent and ranking factors, comprehensive content, relevance to search intent, unique perspectives and case studies.
We're looking at competitor content, and then all of those different steps inform the brief that's the output.
But then when you look at like a net new piece of content or the research, right, we've already got the brief for the assignment.
So that's built into the instructions.
We've already got the artifacts.
We've got different tools.
And then you see how it's got this knowledge-based integration.
It's really meant for this research step so that we can take all those inputs to generate the best research plan.
Who is it for?
What's the objective?
What's the questions we're trying to answer?
And then be
Because it's agentic, it's going to assign a score based on how well the research was done, and then run that in iterations until we reach the right threshold.
And so the knowledge base is going to speed up that process.
That's why it only went through one iteration.
But then it will store the research for the next piece of content that we might write.
Did that make more sense?
But yeah, that was all the context I needed.
I think Cristina's got an additional question there.
No, that makes sense in terms of the knowledge base.
But how does the next step work in terms of feeding back?
Let's say we get to the final version of this, it goes live.
At which point do we start feeding back performance information?
How does it help the system learn?
Let's say this doesn't do very well.
Let's say this doesn't start ranking.
We start making assumptions about why it didn't.
go age.
back.
Where can you provide?
Any information on why it didn't?
Is an update part of it?
Is this something we can fix?
What's kind of like the next step in the feedback loop?
Yeah, for sure.
Well, I mean, it all is really coming from like, which keywords are we targeting?
What's the search volume associated?
What's the keyword difficulty?
And the performance data, right?
Like, which prompts are you showing up in?
Which articles were cited?
Where is there more content engagement?
But we're focused on creating or refreshing five articles per week.
So we're not really trying to like backtrack too much around, hey, why did this specific article not work well?

We're trying to look holistically at where you're showing up, where you're getting cited, and how we can start to create content that addresses all of those different angles, where there is a relevant search volume, right?
So if you take these Augment Code examples, we've created like 300 guides for Augment Code.
And they've had a lot of success in terms of organic traffic with us.
Gone from 20K in June to 60K in December.
We started working with them at the end of June.
Completely stopped their paid advertising as they started to see results through our partnership.
And when you look at searches, you can see that like Augment Code, we have several articles that are similar, but approaching the topic from a different angle.
And you're seeing all of these different articles that we've created showing up.
And so, you know, a lot of it is just like making the right bets and then tailoring the strategy as the data is changing, but not trying to like change an article that we've already published, right?
Just focusing on like, what's the most relevant next article for us to tackle?

We think about these topic clusters in a smart way so that, you know, you have the ability to sort of take all of these different assignments that you can consider that are in our backlog, and we can collaborate using the data and using the different ideas to then move to, like, the next article that we should publish.
So we're sending you five drafts every week, right?
And our only ask is, like, that you review and give feedback on those drafts.
But it's less about, let's take each article and analyze what went right, what went wrong, and it's more so trends and going in the right direction.

So it sounds like you've built a workflow that scales content, right, that we kind of set up, but it doesn't sound like there's yet the ability to feed performance data in to, you know, self, for it to self-learn.
would be through a human...
So cadence, performance cadence with us, right, where we look at the whole and maybe dive deeper is kind of my understanding of this.
And if that's true, I'm just curious to know how does the human interaction work between you and your clients, actually?

Like, what does a kind of team look like and what does your typical operating rhythm look like in terms of, you've already mentioned, kind of you send weekly content for us to review, but like what does it look like in totality in a month, quarter, year?
Yeah, definitely.
So we stand up a Slack channel where we can communicate in real time.
We're active like nearly every day, you know, going back and forth, answering any questions that you have.
We'll do weekly meetings.
We'll review the data on a weekly or bi-weekly cadence.
Monthly, we'll have, you know, like more broader business reviews with you.
And then we'll also have like a Notion workspace setup where we'll have all of these different docs.
We'll have...

The Airtable, right, like ContentOS, where we can constantly, like, change the prioritization of what we're working on and, you know, set up these different views where you can see the content across the different topic clusters or, you know, sort it by different categories.
And the team that you'd be working with, we've got, for every client, you'll have a managing editor who's like the content expert that sees things through end-to-end.
You'll have a content manager who does a lot of the day-to-day communication and execution, the staging and publishing.
And then you'll have a pooled group of managing directors that we don't assign a managing director for each client.

You know, there's a pool of managing directors that are working across different clients, and they are really like your strategic guidance that work hand-in-hand with the content expert who's the managing editor.

I think I asked this last time, but with the design part, you've got a workflow for this as well, which create the ramp example of like a kind of self-gen, have you got like a self-gen kind of image creation workflow that you've set up?
We'll do cover images for every single article that we create as part of our process.
We could also do an image like within the content that could be an infographic or maybe just like a graphic or an image.
Ramps looks quite clean.
It does look like a designer has had some degree of influence on it.
So is there a process where we get a designer who kind of reviews the content in the same way the content editor does?

Like, what's the, I'm just so curious to understand, like, how do your, those individual members interact with AI or are there examples where it's a fully outsourced designer that works on all the images, you know?
Right.
It would.

It would add cost to the engagement, but if you wanted to have custom design or development like Lovable did when we built their templates library, right, and built the functionality around being able to preview the template or remix it and the structure of each of these template pages, if you wanted something like that or something like what we did with Ramp for their vendor directory, it would be like adding costs.
Sure, sure.
But like your workflow would be able to output on-brand relevant images that a designer within the team that we have does X with.
Like, do they just kind of review the images?
Like, what's the involvement of the designer within that squad?
From our team or your team?
Your team, your team.
It said that we had a designer.
I'm sure I saw that.
There was a designer in part of the squad.
Oh, um, in terms of, like, the contract?
Yeah.
Yeah.

So we, yeah, I mean, our team would like review the, you know, they would review the output here and select the image that they think is the best as part of the article that we send to your team to review.
But they wouldn't be doing any like custom design or anything like that.
We'd be using the workflow to generate the images unless you want to have, can they optimize the workflow to produce better images?
It's kind like, I'm just trying to understand like beyond picking an image, what do we get from a designer that would work in that squad?
Or is it just the best image?
Yeah, definitely.

I mean, we're optimizing the workflow to make sure that, right, like the images that are reference images, the, you know, the text around like how we're generating the image, you know, that's all up to speed.
But they're not doing any like custom design or anything like that.
of more like engineers, right?

They're like engineer designers that can help improve the prompting, the workflows, the knowledge library that feeds the image generation workflow kind of thing is kind of the best way to describe that individual skill set.
I think that's a good way to describe it.
Exactly.
Yeah, we're using this for every pipeline where we generate images for clients.
And when we're generating those images, we're using their brand guidelines to inform how the image is generated.

And if your team was like, hey, you know, this doesn't look like our brand, like we don't like these images, then they would be optimizing that workflow so that the image generation meets your standards around your brand guidelines.
Exactly.
But it's a cover image, right?
It's not like the UI of the UI UX of like the actual web page.
Yeah, I'm more interested in like how RAMP, because in many of the RAMP articles, they do have like a kind of, you know, like graphs that have got the right...
You know, they've got the right monetary amount on some of the Ramp ones, and it looks like a designer, not the cover images, but some of these, right?
Some of these ones, see them on Ramp.
They're not necessarily interactive, but they're super relevant to what's in the content.

And, you know, I'm just curious to know, where does that within your service, is that something that you can expect from the workflow, or is that something that there's kind of more of a human-led approach on?
This is a human-led approach.

This is one of our design engineers built this component for them, optimized it for Ramp, and Ramp paid for an additional work stream or an additional amount that was, like, you know, not a small amount, right, to, like, have that as part of their engagement.
This is more custom in nature.
It's not the workflow creating this graph with minimal human involvement.
It's a human, you know, creating this module.
Is a human creating a workflow or a component specific for this that AI produces, or a human just fully?
The custom outsourced design on this, just to be clear.
I'm almost positive that this was pretty much all human-led for Rant and not a workflow specifically.
Yeah, sorry for getting a little confused there.
No, no, all good.
Cristina?
Very much better on this question as well.
How many rounds of feedback do we get until you guys tell us this is extra?

Yeah, because if I'm familiar in any way with the design process, using AI is more that it takes a lot of back and forth until you identify what the right style of image is, and you can build that workflow.
So I guess this goes into phase zero, when you're still building the brand guideline.
How many times can you say, this doesn't look quite right, and you guys will still expand us versions until we need a designer on this?
Oh, yeah, for sure.
I think with images, you know, we're going to like...
Do whatever it takes to get the cover images to a place that you're happy with at the beginning of the partnership.

But with the words, right, with the text, it would be like two rounds of review for an article after we had finished the calibration in phase one, where we're really trying to make sure we hit the quality bar.
And that would be the split between like the design review versus the word, the editorial review.
And could you show us, so both Ramp and Lovable had the additional designer package.
Could you show us a customer that doesn't, that just has the basic workflow, just to see the difference?
Yeah, for sure.
So I would say that for, you know, Engine, right, we've created these business travel guides.
And so, you know.
Engine, we're publishing the cover image for each of these articles.
There's tables sometimes for the content.
That would be a good example.
You know, lovable here, more of these guides, like a table of contents.
Cross-linking.
There's some different examples there.
Awesome.
Conscious of the time, is there anything, Cristina, from your side that you would like to see at this stage, I think, before we conclude?
I think this has been good.
It might be nice to get a bit on the reporting.
Can be like an additional follow-up email.
A bit more on your reporting features, like what exactly do the dashboards look like?
But I know Oli's got like a big question on his mind a little bit about Ramp.
And so, and Oli, you want to go ahead with that, I'm fine with like additional follow-ups.
Yeah, yeah, no.
So I guess to go, to jump a little bit ahead, it's like the ways of working side of things, right?

That we talked about last time, Tyler, with obviously Ramp, Brex, and potentially if you added any of our European competitors into the mix, becomes quite a challenge for us, I think, internally to maybe, it's not particularly palatable, I think, when you've got the same model across so many competitors in one space, particularly if those competitors, unlike Ramp and Brex, who are US, are based in the same territories as us.
It's big kind of, it's the number one blocker, right?
That if I were to talk about this with any, anyone, that'd be like, well, yeah, there's a potential conflict there.

Yeah, so would love to get kind of this point specifically ironed out, I think, before we go too much further into like the granularities of costs, cadencing, reporting, you know, all the things that we can expect of, you that kind of final technical discovery stage, right?
Before we move into pricing, et cetera.

So yeah, that's my only thing is that I don't need an answer right now because I think you've got a policy actually you said on this or guidelines, but that would be the one thing that I get asked, right?
Yeah, makes sense.
I'll go ahead and send you the competitive client policy that we have.
There's like a link to it and it highlights the SOPs where we don't, you know, basically allow the information to mix between.
Or the team members to mix between the two competitors that we would be working for.
But, you it's not uncommon, right?
Ramp and Brex are competitors, lovable, and anything are competitors.
There's a few more examples of competitors that we're working with.
But I think, you know, I think that'd probably be the best first step.
And then I can send more information on reporting so that you can see, like, exactly what we're tracking and how we track it.
Okay.
Great.
What's the good next step from here, Tyler?
Conscious that we, you know, Cristina's joining in January.
We would quite like to kind of nail some things, I think, before Cristina joins as well.
So we can move as quick as kind of the process allows us to, to some extent.
So what does a good next step look like?
Yeah, would say a good next step would be...
Thank
I'll send these resources over, and if there are lingering questions that I haven't answered, feel free to just email me those, and I can answer them over email.
And then after that, if there's more questions that you have, we can set up another call to talk about those in more detail.
I can maybe bring someone to that call if I need support.
And from there, it would just be making sure that you've sent any red lines around the SoW that I sent over.

And I think once, you know, the team is able to, you know, give red lines, or sorry, not red lines, but give a verbal, basically, that you'd like to move forward and that it's really just, you know, ironing out the contract language, then we can reserve, like, our earliest available kickoff, assuming that, you know, the red lines are red lines that we can agree to.
Yeah, okay.
Super helpful.
Super helpful.
All right.
Well, I think let's, yeah, let's create a new email thread between us three.

And if you don't mind kicking that off, Tyler, with this specific point, I guess, on the first question around competitors, then that will allow me to at least circulate this internally before we get to those stages as well.
Because as I say, it's probably the one flagship kind of key point, I think.
Many people would want know when I say that this is Ramps slash Brex's kind of content agency, right?
It'll be a, yeah, it'll be.
Good.
All right.
Thank you so much.
And thanks, Cristina, for joining while you're on your train.
Makes perfect sense.
Yeah.
Thanks, Cristina, for joining.
Oh, sorry.
Go ahead.
Dick Blatt had worked.
So, somewhat.
Congrats on the new role, by the way.
And yeah, let me know if you need anything.
Happy to help and definitely want to make sure you got all the information you need before you have to make a decision.
Happy days.
Thank you, Tyler.
Thank you.
Bye.
Cheers.
Cheers.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Review GrowthX's AI content service for Spendesk.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=172.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Service Model:** GrowthX delivers a "white glove" service, managing the entire content workflow (strategy, creation, publishing) to produce 5 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=683.0)
  - [**Process Foundation:** The workflow is built on four "artifacts" (company, ICP, brand voice, guidelines) created in an 8-week strategic Phase 1.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=189.0)
  - [**Human-Led Optimization:** Performance data (Looker dashboard) is analyzed by GrowthX's team, who manually refine the artifacts to improve future content quality.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=794.0)
  - [**Primary Blocker:** Spendesk is concerned about GrowthX serving direct competitors (Ramp, Brex). GrowthX will provide its competitive client policy to address this.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1937.0)

## Topics

### Spendesk's Content Challenge

  - [Cristina (incoming Head of Content) needs to scale content production from a lean team.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=35.0)
  - [Goal: Reconcile high quality with increased quantity, moving beyond manual ABM workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=155.0)

### GrowthX's Solution: AI-Powered Content Service

  - [**Focus:** SEO-driven editorial content, with programmatic opportunities explored later.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=172.0)
  - [**Phase 1 (8 weeks): Strategy & Calibration**](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=189.0)
      - [Goal: Create four foundational "artifacts" that define the content engine.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=211.0)
      - [Process: Kickoff → product deep dive → CMS deep dive.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=371.0)
      - [Rationale: The system's output quality directly depends on the quality of these inputs.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=199.0)
  - [**Phase 2: Production & Optimization**](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1117.0)
      - [Cadence: 5 articles/week (new or refreshed).](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1174.0)
      - [Feedback: Spendesk provides feedback on drafts (2 rounds post-calibration).](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1760.0)
      - [Optimization: GrowthX's team manually refines the artifacts based on performance data to improve future content quality.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=794.0)

### Workflow & Technology

  - [**Artifacts:** Define the content engine's core identity.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=211.0)
      - [Company (positioning, differentiators)](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=211.0)
      - [ICP/Personas (jobs-to-be-done)](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=489.0)
      - [Brand Voice (tone, style)](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=211.0)
      - [Writing Guidelines (competitor mentions, terminology)](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=521.0)
  - [**Knowledge Graph:** An internal tool for GrowthX's team to accelerate research.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=844.0)
      - [Purpose: Store and connect research findings, not for SEO linking.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=844.0)
      - [Function: Feeds the AI research agent to speed up iterations and improve accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1062.0)

### Team & Communication

  - [**Client Team:**](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1397.0)
      - [Managing Editor: Content expert, end-to-end owner.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1397.0)
      - [Content Manager: Daily comms, execution, publishing.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1397.0)
      - [Pooled Managing Directors: Strategic guidance.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1397.0)
  - [**Communication:**](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1327.0)
      - [Slack channel for daily comms.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1327.0)
      - [Weekly/bi-weekly data review meetings.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1327.0)
      - [Monthly business reviews.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1327.0)
      - [Shared Notion workspace and Airtable ContentOS.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1371.0)

### Design & Visuals

  - [**Standard Service:** AI-generated cover images for all articles.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1440.0)
      - [Process: GrowthX's team selects the best AI-generated image.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1560.0)
      - [Optimization: GrowthX's "designer" role is a prompt engineer who refines workflows based on client feedback to ensure brand alignment.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1619.0)
  - [**Custom Design:** Requires an additional, separate workstream and cost.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1693.0)
      - [Example: Ramp's interactive vendor directory charts were built by a human design engineer.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1671.0)

### Competitive Client Policy

  - [**Issue:** Spendesk is concerned about GrowthX serving direct competitors (Ramp, Brex).](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1937.0)
  - [**GrowthX's Policy:** Strict SOPs prevent information mixing between competing clients.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1937.0)
      - [Example: No team member works on two competing accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2022.0)
  - [**Precedent:** GrowthX successfully serves other competing pairs (e.g., Ramp & Brex).](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2044.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tyler:**](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2093.0)
      - [Share the competitive client policy.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1937.0)
      - [Send details on reporting dashboards.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=1915.0)
      - [Start a new email thread to discuss the policy.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2156.0)
  - [**Spendesk:**](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2073.0)
      - [Review the policy internally.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2156.0)
      - [Email any follow-up questions.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2093.0)
      - [Redline the SoW and provide a verbal go-ahead to reserve a kickoff slot.](https://fathom.video/share/kbqhQGyf7Ck9ZXHZ4xVRMwyaru7i5Dfj?tab=summary&timestamp=2118.0)

action_items.description: Email Ollie & Cristina: competitive client policy + reporting dashboards
Email Tyler verbal go-ahead to proceed
